coredata.dc:title: Nanoparticle-assisted stabilization of metal species as an alternative to conventional approaches for avoiding volatilization errors in total reflection X-ray fluorescence: A review

Romero, Vanesa
Lavilla, Isela
Bendicho, Carlos
coredata.dc:description: Total Reflection X-Ray Fluorescence spectrometry (TXRF) provides many advantages for metal and metalloid analysis. In general, the sample should be prepared as a thin film. To that end, for the analysis of liquid, suspensions or solids previously digested, a small aliquot of the sample is dropped on the sample carrier (e.g. quartz reflector). However, main inconveniences of this technique are the low sensitivity achieved for certain elements depending on their fluorescence yield (also related with the atomic number and the line series selected for the element quantification), and the high volatility of certain elements and compounds (such as oxides, fluorides, chlorides and hydrides) due to their loss during sample drop evaporation performed in the quartz reflector as a consequence of sample preparation before TXRF analysis. Published papers on this subject are mainly related to mercury, but volatilization losses of selenium, arsenic, lead and cadmium and halogens have also been reported. Different analytical approaches have been developed to improve the sample preparation step so as to avoid losses of volatile elements and compounds before TXRF analysis, which are summarized in this review. These strategies include slurry sampling, amalgamation, complexation, adsorption onto membranes, immobilization on quartz reflectors and more recently approaches using nanomaterials like metallic nanoparticles, carbon dots and graphene that can also preconcentrate target analytes. Potentials and pitfalls of these strategies are discussed in this review, as well as other tentative approaches.

Strategies to avoid or reduce volatilization losses (especially for As, Se, Hg)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of the percentage of selected publications for each element using conventional (A) and nanoparticle-assisted stabilization (B) strategies.
Scheme of strategies developed in the literature to reduce volatility losses of metal and metalloid species.

(A) TXRF spectra obtained for several types of NPs using a Mo X-ray tube (Ag NPs, Pd NPs, Fe3O4 NPs and graphene membranes. (B) Scheme of fluorescence energies of TXRF using W and Mo excitation sources, analyte and NPs, indicating the non-useful and useful areas of the spectra).
Selected applications of conventional stabilization strategies.

row.entry: Amalgamation with a gold thin film
High affinity towards mercury. Short amalgamation times (around 15 min).
Strong spectral interference from gold peak. Specific data processing needed for peak deconvolution. Thin film not recyclable.
Amalgamation with a silver thin film
Not overlapping of noble metal (Ag) and mercury fluorescence peaks. Cheaper than gold film.
Thin films must be storage under inert and dry conditions to avoid damages. Thin film not recyclable.
Adsorption by complexation with Nafion®-dithizone membrane
Easy preparation of membranes using commercially available Nafion®. Good membrane adhesion.
Long uptakes times (7 days). Strong interference from potassium and calcium ions. Low adsorption selectivity.
Adsorption by complexation with PVC-dithizone membranes
Good membrane adhesion onto the quartz reflector. No interferences from foreign ions present in water samples.
Membrane suitable only for one use, not possibility of recycling.
Adsorption by complexation with PVC-dithizone membranes
Shorter uptake times using soft stirring during extraction. Good membrane adhesion. Easy removal of the membrane with absolute ethanol.
Use of additives and organic solvent for membrane preparation. Lack of homogeneity of the membrane deposited onto the quartz reflector.
Short uptake times (10 min). High selectivity towards mercury. Feasibility of mercury speciation.
Time-consuming quartz reflector treatment for activation and modification. Lack of recycling.
Trapping onto Cyanex 471× modified quartz reflector
Simpler and cheaper quartz reflector modification. No leaching of ligand during extraction.
Low decrease of mercury volatilization (30% reduction in comparison with untreated samples).
Low recoveries of Hg (around 40%). Rapid decomposition of APDC solutions.
Hg-EDTA complexes are highly efficient for the photocatalytic degradations of mercury.
Non-selectivity of EDTA. Competitive binding in the presence of other cations.
High mercury recoveries reducing in 80% the losses by volatilization.
Trapped mercury is only stable for 60 min stored at room temperature.
Arsenic speciation. Feasibility of multielemental analysis. Removal of sample matrix reducing the background in the spectrum.
Low storage stability. Stable for one week stored at 8 °C.
DLLME in CCl4 after complexing with APDC
Stabilization and pre-concentration of the target element.
Possible losses by spreading of the organic extractant phase when depositing onto the quartz reflector.
DLLME in CCl4 after complexing with APDC
Sensitivity improved by more than two orders of magnitude compared to the direct analysis by TXRF.
Pre-heating of the quartz reflectors (80 °C) is needed for depositing the organic extractant phase.
DSDME of mercury as HgI2 complex in benzene
Formation of small concentrated thin films (Ø 3 mm) onto the quartz reflector increasing the sensitivity.
Solvent exchange from organic to aqueous solvent needed. Analytical procedure involves two drying steps.
Reduction with ascorbic acid and simultaneous adsorption onto quartz reflector.
Simple, cost-effective and selective to Se(IV).
Strategy less effective for other volatile elements (Hg, Pb) limiting the multielemental analysis.
Ultrasonic-probe sampling solution with 0.01% m/v Triton X-100
Increased recoveries compared to extraction in acidic medium. Simple treatment. Deposition of a homogenous thin film onto the quartz reflector.
Deposition of solid particles could increase the background in the spectrum hindering the sensitivity.

head.ce:title: Nanoparticle-assisted stabilization of metal species as an alternative to conventional approaches for avoiding volatilization errors in total reflection X-ray fluorescence: A review

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Total Reflection X-Ray Fluorescence spectrometry (TXRF) provides many advantages for metal and metalloid analysis. In general, the sample should be prepared as a thin film. To that end, for the analysis of liquid, suspensions or solids previously digested, a small aliquot of the sample is dropped on the sample carrier (e.g. quartz reflector). However, main inconveniences of this technique are the low sensitivity achieved for certain elements depending on their fluorescence yield (also related with the atomic number and the line series selected for the element quantification), and the high volatility of certain elements and compounds (such as oxides, fluorides, chlorides and hydrides) due to their loss during sample drop evaporation performed in the quartz reflector as a consequence of sample preparation before TXRF analysis. Published papers on this subject are mainly related to mercury, but volatilization losses of selenium, arsenic, lead and cadmium and halogens have also been reported. Different analytical approaches have been developed to improve the sample preparation step so as to avoid losses of volatile elements and compounds before TXRF analysis, which are summarized in this review. These strategies include slurry sampling, amalgamation, complexation, adsorption onto membranes, immobilization on quartz reflectors and more recently approaches using nanomaterials like metallic nanoparticles, carbon dots and graphene that can also preconcentrate target analytes. Potentials and pitfalls of these strategies are discussed in this review, as well as other tentative approaches.

ce:section.ce:para: Total Reflection X-Ray Fluorescence spectrometry (TXRF) provides several advantages, such as simultaneous multielemental analysis, suitability for dealing with microsamples, no consumption of argon or cooling water for refrigeration, decreased background noise and matrix effects, wide linear range and easy calibration [1,2]. Currently, TXRF can be applied to a huge variety of samples (human tissue samples, rat brain tissue, cancer cell tissues, particulate matter, cloud water, clays, contaminated water, ambient aerosols, soils, sand, nuclear materials, cosmetics, fuels) [3–13], being in certain occasions an alternative to inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES), electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry ETAAS or conventional X-ray fluorescence (XRF).

TXRF has been increasingly applied to micro- and trace analysis, involving different sample preparation procedures depending on the type of sample. To perform an analysis under total-reflection conditions is mandatory to provide the samples as a thin film on the surface of the sample reflector (e.g. quartz reflector) thus reducing matrix effects. In previous published reviews on TXRF, several sample preparation procedures were described [12,14,15]. To sum up, for the preparation of liquid samples and suspensions or solid samples previously digested, a small aliquot (few microliters) of the sample is placed on the quartz reflector and the solvent is dried until evaporation by heating or under vacuum to obtain a thin film of a few nanometers thick of sample remained adhered to the quartz support. However, the low sensitivity achieved by this technique continues being an inconvenience and can occurred mainly due to two reasons: i) firstly, due to the low excitation feasibilities and fluorescence yields, and depend on the atomic number and the line series used for the element quantification. Usually, lower sensitivity is achieved for light elements (with lower atomic number) and L-lines (more sensitive for K-lines) and ii) secondly, due to the sample preparation, which is the subject of this review and it is especially remarkable for elements or compounds with high volatility due to their loss during sample drop drying/evaporation performed on the quartz reflector before TXRF analysis.

A classification of volatile elements and compounds can be made in: i) elements (gaseous element, Te, Sn, Pb, Tl, P, As, Sb, S, Se, Br, I, Zn, Cd, Hg); ii) oxides of As, S, Se, Te, Re, Ru, Os, Zn, Cd and Hg; iii) fluorides of B, Si, Ge, Sn, O, As, Sb, Bi, S, Se, Te, Ti, Zr, Hg, V, Nb, Ta, Mo, W, Re, Ru, Os, Ir and Hg; iv) chlorides of Al, Ga, In, Tl, Ge, Sn, Pb, As, Sb, Bi, S, Se, Te, Ti, Zr, Hf, Ce, V, Nb, Ta, Mo, W, Mn, Fe, Ru, Os, Au, Zn, Cd, Hg and v) hydrides of Si, Ge, Sn, Pb, P, As, Sb, Bi, S, Se and Te based on a previous publication [16].

Those volatile elements with high vapor pressure at room temperatures and low boiling point, such as Hg, Se or Cd among others could be lost by volatilization. Furthermore, some elements or compounds of Hg, Se, As, Pb, Zn, Cd, Ge, etc. can be lost by the increase in temperature or during evaporation of solutions, for example, in the drying step or in decomposition procedures, for example by acid digestion, because they can be present as several chemical forms (oxides, halides and hydrides) that suffer partial or total volatilization or decomposition [16]. These errors can be reduced by using closed digestion systems and working at low temperatures [16]. For instance, Custo et al. [17] reported that volatilization (due to high vapor pressure) and decomposition of metallic Hg and other Hg compounds (mercury chlorides, nitrates and oxide) occurring at temperatures of 70–85 °C prevented Hg quantification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reported problems of volatility in TXRF
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the main problems related to low analyte recovery reported in the literature derived from the mentioned volatile elements and compounds were described, as well as how researchers observed and what experiments researchers performed to detect loss of volatile elements and preliminary studies on how to avoid the analyte losses. In general, researchers observed low recoveries using spikes or reference materials, low sensitivity (inability to deal with low concentrations of these elements) and poor reproducibility (showing high relative standard deviation values). Volatility problems were previously reported in TXRF especially for Hg [12,17–19], but also for As [20], Se [12,21,22], Ge [22], Pb and Zn [23]. Typically in TXRF, calibration is performed by adding an internal standard to the sample solution (Ga, Ge or Y). However, elements commonly used as internal standard such as Ge, should be avoided when HCl at acid pH was used due to the formation of volatile chlorocomplexes of Ge, which are volatilized during drying step [22,24,25]. Even, for the preparation of element standards, HCl should be prevented. Messerschmidt et al. [26] performed several studies with As, Se, Sb and Bi standard solutions to evaluate analyte losses by volatilization. For standard solutions in 1 M HCl, recoveries of 6, 75 and 91% were obtained for Se, As and Sb, respectively, indicating analyte losses especially for Se due to the high volatility.

The comparison of calibration curves of potential volatile elements can be performed without and with a complexing agent in the solution allowed detecting losses of volatile elements. For instance, the comparison of standard addition calibration curves for Hg in the presence or absence of EDTA in tap water and fruit juices pointed that the use of EDTA complexing agent (EDTA: sample solution, 1:3) allowed increasing the calibration slope, improving the linearity of the calibration curve and the detection limit, indicating the necessity to apply a strategy for Hg trapping owing to the loss of the volatile Hg produced during the evaporation process [27].

Additionally, the study of drying/evaporation drop for standard solutions or samples allow evaluating analyte losses. For example, different drying procedures (e.g., vacuum, hot plate at 100 °C and infrared lamp) were assessed for drop evaporation of Hg standard solutions at different concentration levels (1 mg L-1, 100 μg L−1 and 10 μg L−1) [18]. In the three evaluated forms of drying, partial volatilization was observed due to a low recovery that varied in the range of 30% (for the lower Hg concentration) and 60% (for the higher Hg concentrations) [18]. Margui et al. [12] also found volatilization problems during the drying step of a body cream sample on the quartz reflector that hampered Hg. In fact, a Hg concentration of 2.33 ± 0.81 mg Kg-1 as reference value could not be detected by TXRF due to the Hg losses. Furthermore, Staniszewski and Freimann observed a decrease in the concentration of As in lengthy sample preparation and measurement procedures that could be attributed to a decrease of As on the quartz reflector as a result of the production of volatile arsenic compounds [20]. Other researchers found recoveries of 50% for As, the As losses being attributed to the evaporation of solutions containing inorganic As on quartz reflectors, which produced the formation and sublimation of arsenious acid nanoparticles [28].

Besides, digestion and dry ashing methods generally utilized to prepare solid samples prior to TXRF analysis can also cause the loss of volatile elements [29] in addition to other problems such as contamination, analyte dilution and time consuming operation. Open vessel acid digestion especially increases the volatility of metals and metalloids due to the formation of volatile halides and the use of acids with boiling points, such as HNO3 (120 °C), aqua regia (108 °C) and HNO3:H2O2 (85 °C) in open systems. A huge number of publications reported metal and metalloid losses, especially in open vessel acid digestion, but also in microwave ovens for other techniques [30,31]. Some examples were found in acid digestion followed by TXRF analysis. Messerschmidt et al. [26] developed a procedure based on digestion, hydride generation and collection followed by dilution and drop deposition onto the carrier for As, Se, Sb and Bi determination. Recovery studies of these elements in reference materials of plants and biological tissues after acid digestion in open quartz tubes (HNO3 + HClO4), dilution (in HCl solution), hydride generation and collection in HCl/HNO3 diluted solution showed recovery values of 60–95% for As, 70% for Bi, 5–10% for Se, and 20–40% for Sb. The low recoveries were attributed to incomplete hydride generation and incomplete collection for Sb, and to the high volatility in the case of Se. Likewise, concentration of 15 elements was determined in beer samples directly and after acid digestion in a microwave oven with HNO3/H2O2. Low recovery was also detected for As in digested beer samples due to volatilization in comparison with direct analysis of samples [32]. Rodella et al. [23] obtained low recoveries for Zn and Pb in municipal solid waste incineration fly ash. They justified the differences to sample variability and possible volatilization during sample digestion (HNO3 + HCl + HF) of lead and zinc chlorides present in the sample due to their relatively high vapor pressure and low boiling points [23].

Gama et al. [29] observed a lower concentration of chloride and bromide because acid digestion is not adequate for these elements due to the formation of acids HCl and HBr, which are lost by volatilization.

Thus, the direct analysis of samples containing these species without further treatment or after acid digestion is not adequate, because a significant amount of them is lost through evaporation mainly occurring during the drying step obtaining a poor recovery. More importantly, it is especially recommended to obtain good limits of detection (LODs) in drinking water samples for these elements, since the maximum allowed content is 1 μg L−1 for Hg, 5 μg L−1 for Pb, Cd and Sb and 10 μg L−1 for As and Se according to European Commission, 2015 [33].

In the early years of the development of TXRF, very few papers focused on the volatilization problem during sample preparation were reported. In fact, the first reports where Hg was quantified by TXRF were published in 1996 using a complexing agent [34] and in 1997 using a silver coating to minimize losses due to evaporation [35]. At present, several approaches are available to improve the sample preparation step so as to prevent losses of volatile elements before TXRF analysis. Reported works are mostly related to Hg, and a lesser amount of papers deal with volatile species of Se, As, Pb and Cd as can be observed in Fig. 1 .

Thus, the main goal of this review is to highlight the problems of volatile species in sample preparation prior to TXRF analysis and to provide a general overview of the different strategies published in the literature to avoid or reduce evaporation losses of volatile metal and metalloid species. Trapping and preconcentration methods, including the use of chelating agents, membranes, as well as the use of nanoparticles (NPs) are reviewed describing their advantages and drawbacks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strategies to avoid or reduce volatilization losses (especially for As, Se, Hg)
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, generally for the preparation of liquid samples and suspension before TXRF analysis, a small aliquot of the sample is placed on a quartz reflector and dried before analysis. The drying step can be performed by different ways, e.g. in a heating plate, under vacuum, with an infrared lamp or in an oven. The drying step affects not only to the thickness and homogeneity of the dry sample, but also promotes loss of volatile analytes which would worsen the sensitivity and accuracy of the analytical approach. High drying temperatures can cause the decomposition of the elements or volatilization of formed species [17,25], such as halides (e.g. HgCl2, GeCl4), oxides (HgO) or nitrates (HgNO3). Also, when drying under vacuum the saturation temperature decreases promoting the volatilization of the target analyte [36].

At present, different strategies have been reported in order to stabilize and minimize losses of the target volatile analytes during sample preparation before TXRF analysis. The strategies for trapping volatile metal and metalloid species and to reduce volatilization risks have been classified in two: i) conventional stabilization strategies and ii) nanoparticle-assisted stabilization strategies. Fig. 2 shows a scheme of the published strategies.

Reported works without using NPs are mainly related to Hg (around 50% of reported works). Other less studied volatile elements are Se, As, Pb and Cd. Proposed approaches to reduce losses of volatile metal species include amalgamation with noble metals [35,37], adsorption onto membranes [38–40], immobilization with specific ligands [18,41], complexation in solution [17–20,34,42], liquid-liquid microextraction [22,43,44], reduction [21] and slurry sampling [4].
In this section the different strategies reported without using NPs for sample preparation of volatile elements prior to analysis by TXRF are discussed. Table 1 summarizes selected applications.

This strategy was proposed for Hg trapping onto very thin films of noble metals forming thermal stable amalgams [35,37]. For this purpose, a thin film of Au [37] or Ag [35] is deposited onto the quartz reflector. Then, it is placed in direct contact with the sample solution by stirring or direct dropping of the sample onto the thin film. After amalgamation, the quartz reflector is washed and dried in a laminar flow hood before TXRF analysis.

Greaves et al. [35] demonstrated that it is crucial to control the noble metal film thickness in order to avoid the migration of the amalgamated Hg to the bulk of the coating, which would affect the stability as a function of time. Very thin films of Ag (thickness around 50 nm) were deposited in the center of the quartz reflector by sputtering. Furthermore, short times (~ 15 min) were needed for amalgamation using thin films. Other advantage with this strategy is that the fluorescence intensity of Ag coating can be used as internal standard thus simplifying the sample preparation for quantitative analysis. In this way, the relative signal intensity as a function of Lα lines (Hg Lα / Ag Lα) is obtained. Besides, the amalgamated Hg proved to be stable for at least three months and hence, this approach could be useful for storing the sample for long periods of time before TXRF analysis.

However, there are also many disadvantages inherent with this approach. Caution must be taken during storing of the prepared quartz reflectors (with or without Hg) since noble metals thin films can be damaged by humidity. Also, it should be noted that the production of these films is expensive and after amalgamation, recyclability is not affordable and therefore, it is suitable for only one use. New thin films should be prepared for each amalgamation cycle increasing the costs. Although thin films were used, the concentration of Au or Ag is quite high resulting in intense fluorescence signals, which increases the background of the spectra affecting the LOD. LODs of 15 μg L−1 and 100 μg L−1 were obtained using a Ag thin film and Au thin film, respectively [35,45]. This could limit the analytical applications of this strategy. In addition, when Au thin film is used for Hg amalgamation, fluorescence peaks of Hg and Au overlap in the TXRF spectra, a specific data processing procedure being necessary for peak deconvolution [45].

Similar to the previous strategy, polymeric membranes containing complexing reagents were proposed as thin films for the adsorption of volatile elements onto the surface of the quartz reflector. The main advantage in comparison with pure noble metal films is that using polymeric membranes, there are no critical spectral interferences due to their organic nature thus resulting in lower background. To achieve an efficient adsorption of the target elements, a complexing reagent must be embedded in the membrane matrix. The complexing agent should be chosen as a function of the analyte. Briefly, for preparing the membranes, the selected complexing agent is mixed with the membrane precursor so as to obtain a stable and homogeneous mixture. Then, an aliquot of the mixture is deposited onto the center of a quartz reflector and dried, which results in the formation of a polymeric thin film. For adsorption of the analyte, the modified quartz reflector is directly immersed in the aqueous sample matrix and left for some time. The adhesion of the membrane during the drying step must be good to ensure the absence of leaching when placed in contact with the sample matrix. Using this strategy, different membrane compositions have been tested for Hg and Pb adsorption [38–40].

Koulouridakis et al. [39] reported the preparation of a Nafion®-dithizone based membrane for Pb adsorption. In this work, Nafion® was selected as a precursor since it is commercially available in liquid form, thus simplifying the membrane preparation. Besides, Nafion® displays hydrophilic channels that could facilitate the diffusion and transfer of the target element inside the membrane [46]. This material provides good adhesion to quartz surface and can be easily removed with absolute ethanol. Although Nafion® already contains sulphur groups that can complex Pb cations, the adsorption efficiency is improved in 70% when dithizone is included in the membrane composition [39]. Good sensitivity is achieved with a LOD of 0.5 μg L−1 Pb, but a disadvantage is the long time required for reaching equilibrium conditions (7 days). Furthermore, it has been demonstrated that using Nafion®-based membranes, Ca and K cations are potential interferences that block the actives sites thereby preventing the adsorption of the target analyte [38]. In addition, the accumulation of high contents of Ca and K would lead to the saturation of the detector due to the strong fluorescence signals. Thus, poly(vinyl chloride)-dithizone (PVC-dithizone) membranes were proposed as improved selective substrates to diminish the interferences. However, it should be noted that the preparation of these membranes implies the use of more additives and an organic solvent, which increases the cost per membrane. These PVC-dithizone membranes have been applied for Hg retention [38,40]. Margui et al. [40] showed that Hg extraction rate can be improved by stirring the sample. Under stirring at 120 rpm, extraction efficiency was improved around two times in comparison with the extraction operation performed without stirring. However, same authors also shown that stirring conditions should be carefully controlled since high speeds originates strong waves affecting the contact between the sample and the membrane hampering the adsorption process. Under optimal conditions, a LOD for Hg of 0.3 μg L−1 was obtained. The method allowed the determination of Hg in spiked sea, tap and river water samples without analyte losses. Nevertheless, the main disadvantage of using membranes is the lack of homogeneity onto the quartz reflector. In the case of PVC-dithizone membranes, functional groups are preferentially located at the border in the formed membranes due to liquid spread after dropping the membrane mixture on the quartz reflector. The analyte distribution on the membrane after adsorption is inhomogeneous, so the diameter of the membrane spot must not be higher than 10 mm to ensure the complete exposure of the membrane to the X-ray beam during analysis [40]. Although the cost per membrane is not high (i.e. few μL of reaction mixture and low temperatures are needed for generating the functionalized membranes), it should be noted that membranes are not reusable, hence increasing the waste generation. Furthermore, there are not results showing the quartz reflector storage stability. Further studies are clearly needed to develop more homogeneous and reproducible membranes on the surface of quartz reflectors as well as the possibility of reusing them to reduce costs and waste generation.

Direct immobilization of specific ligands on the surface of quartz reflector can provide homogeneous distribution of active sites for the retention of the target analyte. This strategy was developed for Hg stabilization before TXRF analysis [18,41]. Aretaki et al. [41] reported the immobilization of 4-(2-pyridylazo-resorcinol) as ligand for trapping Hg. Quartz reflector surfaces need to be activated with 1,1‑carbonyldiimidazole (CDI) prior to immobilization, which reacts with the hydroxyl groups of the substrate. After that, the specific ligand (i.e. 4-(2-pyridylazo-resorcinol)) was covalently coupled providing a functionalized quartz reflector, which is chemically and thermally stable. For extraction, the quartz reflector was directly immersed in the sample matrix and finally analyzed by TXRF. No losses of Hg were observed and a LOD of 0.5 μg L−1 could be obtained using an equilibration time of 10 min for the analysis of drinking water. Negligible influence of foreign elements, such as Ni, Ca, As and Cu was observed showing the high selectivity of the selected ligand towards Hg. However, the main disadvantage of the approach is the time-consuming quartz reflector treatment and the lack of recycling.

A simplified procedure was proposed by Marguí et al. [18] using tri-isobutylphosphine (Cyamex 471×) as ligand for Hg trapping. In this case, activation of the quartz reflector is not needed, thus simplifying the procedure. Briefly, an aliquot (10 μL) of Cyanex 471× dissolved in ethanol was placed in the center of the quartz reflector and left to dry at room temperature. Although there is no leaching of Cyanex 471× during the extraction process, this procedure only allows a reduction in 30% of Hg losses when compared to untreated samples. This can be attributed to the lack of stability of the ligand during the drying process prior to TXRF analysis.

Since the use of complexing reagents improves the extraction efficiency, direct complexation of the target analyte by addition of the reagent in the sample matrix has been reported as an efficient approach to reduce losses by volatilization and to storage the samples. This approach is based on the formation of a non-volatile stable chelated coordination complex of the target element with an organic molecule in solution and it has been applied for the determination of Hg [17–19,34], Se [42] and As [20] by TXRF.

Different complexing agents have been tried to reduce volatilization and retain volatile elements quantitatively onto the quartz reflector. In this way, sodium diethyldithiocarbamate (NaDDTC) combined with ammonium pyrrolidine dithiocarbamate (APDC) [34], ethylendiaminetetraaceetic acid (EDTA) [17,19], dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA) [19] and thiourea [18] are among the most used complexing agents. Briefly, the complexing solution is added to the sample and the mixture is left for a period of time for complexing the target analyte. Then, an aliquot of the mixture is transferred to the quartz reflector and dried for TXRF analysis.

First work related to Hg complexation prior TXRF analysis was reported by Hołyńska et al. [34]. In this work, a mixture of NaDDTC and APDC was used for complexing followed by extraction with methyl isobuthyl ketone (MIBK). However, with the proposed methodology, only 40% of Hg was recovered from spiked samples. Furthermore, APDC and NaDDTC solutions decompose rapidly being an inconvenience for the treatment of large number of samples.

Other complexing agents for Hg trapping, such as EDTA and DMSA, have been recently tested [17,19]. Efficiency of EDTA and DMSA has been clearly proved by comparing the recoveries obtained for the samples treated with the complexing agents and the untreated samples. For instance, results obtained by Holtkamp et al. [19] showed that when the sample is not treated with a complexing agent, ca. 80% of the Hg is lost during sample preparation. However, using EDTA or DMSA for trapping Hg, recoveries close to 100% were obtained demonstrating the efficiency in hindering the volatilization of the sample. Beyond the reduction of mercury volatilization, Hg-EDTA complexes have proved to be very efficient for the photocatalytic reduction of inorganic Hg [17]. In the presence of EDTA, 94% of Hg(II) is reduced within 120 min in comparison with the 30% of Hg(II) photoreduction in the absence of EDTA. The photocalytic process can easily be monitored by TXRF analysis in order to control the photoreduction process by determining non-reduced Hg(II) in the remaining solution. Despite EDTA shows a high complexing constant with Hg(II) (Ke = 21.6, 25 °C) [47], the lack of selectivity if this complexing agent could hinder its application for the analysis of environmental samples where other cations are present resulting in a competitive binding.

Thiourea was also reported as an efficient complexing agent for reducing drastically Hg losses by volatilization in 60% when compared to untreated samples [18]. Since mercury shows high affinity towards thiol groups, a low concentration of thiourea (10 mM) is needed for complexing the target analyte at trace level, which diminishes the background in TXRF in comparison with the use of non-selective complexing agents.

In addition to Hg, there are other volatile elemental species of As and Se, which can be lost during the drying step before TXRF analysis. Although few studies have been performed with those elements, it has been proved that Se has a similar behavior as Hg [21,22]. Arsenic in the form of As(III) can also be lost by volatilization during the drying process or when quartz reflectors are stored for long periods of time [48]. Regarding the complexation approach, sodium dibenzyldithiocarbamate (NaDBC) was tested for both elements as trapping agent before TXRF analysis [20,42]. The formation of Se and As chelates with NaDBC followed by automated solid phase extraction allows their determination in a complex matrix such as seawater. The procedure involves trapping of target analytes by forming stable complexes and removing the salt matrix that would prevent the formation of a thin layer onto the quartz reflector being unsuitable for TXRF analysis. Stability studies of As-DBC complex performed by Staniszewski et al. [20] at room temperature and at 8 °C, showed that samples are more stable when stored at lower temperature. Under these conditions sample are stable for one week.

Liquid-liquid microextraction (LLME) was introduced as a miniaturization of the conventional liquid-liquid extraction, in which few microliters are required for extracting the analytes [49]. LLME can be easily combined with TXRF since low sample volumes are needed for measurement. In addition, when LLME is combined with complexing reagents, enrichment and stabilization of volatile elements is achieved reducing losses by volatilization and enhancing the sensitivity. This strategy was used for Se [22], Cd [43], Sb [50] and Hg [44] prior to TXRF analysis.

For Se [22], Sb [50] and Cd [43], dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction (DLLME) was combined with TXRF for ultra-trace determination of these elements in water samples. DLLME is based on the formation of a cloudy solution by mixing an extraction organic solvent (few microliters) with a disperser solvent into an aqueous sample. After rapid injection and vigorous agitation, the fine droplets of the organic phase are well-dispersed through the aqueous sample matrix thus allowing the interaction with the target analyte [51]. After extraction, the mixture is centrifuged and the small volume of organic phase is collected and measured for analyte quantification. The organic solvent used for DLLME should have higher density than water (to facilitate the separation by centrifugation), good affinity towards the target analyte and low solubility in water. Furthermore, a disperser solvent being highly soluble in both organic and aqueous phases is crucial to decrease the interfacial tension between both extraction phases so that the formation of small droplets is feasible.

In order to stabilize volatile analytes for TXRF analysis, the use of an appropriate of disperser solvent and chelating agent extraction can improve extraction yield and reduce losses during sample pre-treatment. Thus, ammonium pyrrolidine dithiocarbamate (APDC) as chelating agent mixed with methanol or ethanol (disperser solvent) and carbon tetrachloride (extraction solvent) were used for DLLME of Se, Sb and Cd. Under optimal conditions, LODs of 1.1 μg L−1, 5 μg L−1 and 0.04 μg L−1 were obtained for Se, Sb and Cd, respectively. Margui et al. [43] demonstrated that the combination of DLLME and TXRF spectrometry can offer a simple, fast (less than 5 min needed for extraction) and low-cost approach compared to hyphenated atomic techniques. However, it should be noted the combination of DLLME and TXRF is not free of drawbacks. DLLME requires an organic solvent as extractant and is this phase, which need to be placed in the quartz reflector for TXRF analysis. Efficient deposition of an organic drop on the quartz reflector is not easy since organic solvent spreads rapidly out of the detection area (Ø 10 mm) yielding loss of sensitivity. To ensure a well-centered thin film on the quartz reflector, Margui et al. [43] tried different strategies, e.g. i) deposition on preheated quartz reflectors; ii) drying under an IR-lamp after deposition and iii) deposition using a PTFE funnel-shaped device fitting the measuring area. Results showed that the most effective procedure was preheating of the quartz reflector at around 80 °C in a hot plate.

Another option to minimize problems of solvent spreading on the quartz reflector is the replacement of the extractant phase for a more polar solvent. In this way, Oskolok et al. [44] develop a procedure based on the directly suspended droplet microextraction (DSDME) of Hg in the form of an iodide molecular complex with benzene as extraction solvent. Before TXRF measurement, solvent exchange was done. Briefly, after extraction, the organic phase containing the HgI2 complex was recovered and heated at 50 °C until dryness. The obtained residue was re-dissolved in 70 μL of an aqueous solution containing 0.01% KI. Finally, an aliquot of the aqueous phase was placed over a siliconized quartz reflector in order to obtain a concentrated thin film with a diameter of 3 mm. Despite good sensitivity is achieved (LOD of 0.02 μg L−1 Hg) with this approach, solvent exchange implies two more steps in the analytical procedure. Moreover, the formed Hg-complex must be thermally stable since increased risk of analyte volatilization occurs as a result of two drying steps required.

Although LLME shows to be easily combined with TXRF for pre-concentrating volatile elements reducing losses during sample preparation, in the last years, solvent-free microextraction techniques based on the use of nanomaterials have been also proposed as efficient and greener strategies for different volatile elements. These approaches are discussed in next sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other sample treatments: slurry sampling, extraction on alumina, increase of viscosity and alkaline standard
ce:section.ce:para: Apart from liquid-liquid microextraction and complexation, other effective sample treatments that can reduce the losses of Se prior TXRF analysis include reduction in solution [21], slurry sampling [4] and extraction onto alumina [52].

Aretaki et al. [21] developed a procedure based on the simultaneous reduction and adsorption of colloidal elemental Se onto the quartz reflector using ascorbic acid as reducing agent. To accomplish the adsorption, the quartz reflector needs to be directly immersed in the aqueous sample matrix. After adding the reducing agent, the mixture was stirred for 45 min at room temperature. Then, the quartz reflector was washed with water and dried using an IR lamp without vaporization of Se followed by TXRF analysis. The procedure was simple, cost-effective and selective for Se(IV). Besides, its applicability for the determination of Se in seawater with a detection limit of 0.8 μg L−1 Se was shown. However, ascorbic acid is much less effective for other volatile elements such as Hg or Pb, which limits the multielemental applicability of this strategy. Besides, samples containing Au at ppb level could be troublesome to analyze since there is a strong spectral interference as a result of Au(III) reduction by ascorbic acid and further adsorption on the surface of the quartz reflector.

Another approach that can also be considered for reducing losses of Se during solid sample treatment is slurry sampling. Ultrasonic-probe slurry sampling (UPSS) was proposed by de la Calle et al. [4] for the multielemental analysis of biological tissues by TXRF. UPSS is based on the formation of a stable and homogeneous solid dispersion (slurry) using a surfactant and sonication. After 30 s of sonication with an ultrasound probe, an aliquot of the resulting slurry was placed onto a quartz reflector and directly analyzed by TXRF after drying. Although this approach was not addressed to reduce Se losses by volatilization, it was observed that recoveries for Se were close to 100% for all analyzed samples without losses by volatilization during the drying step. Besides, ultrasonic assisted extraction (UAE) was also used for comparison. Briefly, a mixture of 10% HNO3 and 40% HCl was added to the solid sample and sonicated for 15 min. After that, the supernatant was separated by centrifugation. Then, an aliquot of the supernatant was placed onto a quartz reflector and dried prior to analysis. Following UAE, Se signal clearly decreased and recoveries around 70% were obtained. This could be attributed to the lack of complete extraction but probably, there would be Se losses by volatilization both during sonication (very long times and highly acidic conditions) and drying operations. This comparison highlights the usefulness of UPSS as a suitable technique for the preparation of solid samples prior to multielemental analysis including volatile elements such as Se.

The use of alumina as solid phase to perform the extraction and preconcentration of As from ultrapure water was also attempted [52]. In a similar way to slurry sampling, alumina was suspended directly onto the aqueous sample with the objective of trapping As by stirring for 24 min. After that, the alumina phase was separated by centrifugation and washed. Then, an aliquot of the wet alumina (ca. 3 mm in the center of the reflector) was directly analyzed due to the characteristics of TXRF. The LOD obtained was 0.8 μg L−1 As with an enrichment factor of 10. Retention of As on alumina prevented volatilization problems and recoveries in the range of 95–98% were achieved. In spite of the problems of reproducibility expected for the slurry preparation and sample treatment, RSD values were lower than 5%. Al, Co or even external calibration can be used, since alumina matrix did not affect the TXRF measurement. This procedure avoids the elution step but centrifugation is still required for separation of alumina-As from the aqueous solution. The work with alumina masses lower than 10 mg was troublesome due to inefficient centrifugation. Additionally, the extraction temperature employed was 47 °C and special attention had to be paid, since high temperature enhanced the adsorption kinetics but risks of analyte losses increased.

Other strategies tried to increase the sample viscosity adding a surfactant (e.g. Triton X-114) [18] or the oxidation with ammonium persulfate [19]. However, these procedures did not provide satisfactory results. On the one hand, Triton X-114 was added to the sample solution in order to increase the viscosity and reduce the volatility, but this did not reduce Hg losses by volatilization and the background increased as compared to untreated samples, which makes difficult the peak identification. On the other hand, the addition of ammonium persulfate for oxidation showed a slight positive effect on the stability of Hg, but 25% of the analyte was lost during the drying step. Both strategies were not further applied since they were not acceptable for a reliable quantification of Hg.

Furthermore, as commented above, the determination of halide ions (Cl, Br and I) is not possible after acid digestion, but also the addition of internal standard (Ga) typically in HNO3 acid due to the exchange of the cation for a proton resulting in the formation of the corresponding volatile hydrogen halide [53,54]. External calibration curve without addition of internal standard solution could be a successful solution [54]. Hoogerstraet et al. [53,55] found that the use of an alkaline standard, for example Cu(NH3)(NO3)2 as internal standard allow the determination of Cl, Br and I avoiding evaporation as HCl, HBr and HI. Also, digestion of supplement tablets with NH3 instead of acids produced a lower residue and allowed drying of the sample at lower temperature (40 °C) reducing evaporation. Besides, the addition of AgNO3 allowed the precipitation of AgI preventing losses of volatile iodine in mineral waters during sample drop evaporation [56]. A similar approach based on the formation of a precipitate of AgCl, by addition of an excess AgNO3 allowed the determination of the Ag content in the filtrate by TXRF doing the measurements in vacuum [57]. Another interesting example is the procedure developed by Dhara et al. [58] for the determination of Cl in nuclear materials. Following the proposed strategy the Cl present in nuclear fuels was firstly separated by pyrohydrolysis as HCl, and then it was collected in NaOH diluted solution (5 mM) avoiding loss of hydrogen chloride. In the case of F, the volatilization as HF produced by acidification should be specially avoided because damage of the sample carrier occurred. Thus, neutral pH should be used using V5+ in the form of VO3 − as internal standard [59].

Recently, many researchers replaced the use of traditional materials by nanomaterials (NMs) due to the promising advantages they provided. NMs have been used in Analytical Chemistry, mainly in sample preparation as sorbent phases for analyte separation, removal of interferences and increase of sensitivity and selectivity due to their physicochemical properties as compared to traditional materials, such as nanoscale dimensions, high surface-to-volume ratio, increased surface activity, easy dispersibility in water and enhanced sorption ability [60,61]. The current trend focused on the combination of the use of NMs and microextraction techniques for trapping metal, metalloids and speciation at trace and ultra-trace levels [62,63].

Since 2013, different NMs have been tested in sample preparation analysis for TXRF. One of the goals pursued in most works was the improvement of LODs and the retention of analytes with volatility problems during drying/evaporation steps on the quartz reflector. Thus, different techniques used with NPs has derived from traditional liquid –liquid extraction (LLE) and solid-phase extraction (SPE). Firstly, a description of main techniques has been included.

For analyte pre-concentration and matrix removal, SPE is one of the most popular techniques due to its simplicity and low cost in terms of solvent and time. Classical SPE has evolved into miniaturized modern techniques following a drastic reduction of the amount of organic solvent or solid phase. The developed techniques were for example dispersive micro-solid phase extraction (D-μ-SPE) and thin-film microextraction (TFME).

D-μ-SPE is mainly applied in static mode, where the extraction phase (NPs) is dispersed in the sample solution for a certain time. Then, the sorbent phase (in the range of μg or mg) is separated from the sample matrix that could be performed following several strategies: a) decantation, b) filtration, c) centrifugation or d) magnetic separation (for magnetic NPs). Additional, washing to eliminate unretained compound is performed. Then, there are also three main options to follow: a) elution of retained analytes by adding the adequate solvent (diluted HNO3), b) direct analysis of the dispersed NPs (especially interesting in TXRF) or c) resuspension of NPs in a large volume and further analysis. The use of NPs allows obtaining a better dispersion in the sample solution and increased sorption of target analytes onto this phase [64,65]. However, loss of NPs can occurs during filtration or centrifugation especially for carbon nanotubes (CNTs).

TFME involves the use of a thin film or membrane as sorbent phase [66]. This strategy can be applied in two modes: headspace (HS-TFME) and direct immersion (DI-TFME). For using NMs as extraction phase in TFME, they were configured as thin films with large surface area and high extraction efficiency. In DI-TFME, the membrane is directly suspended in the sample solution whereas in HS-TFME, the NPs thin film is placed in the headspace of sample solution allowing the migration of volatile species and retention on the film.

Most of these strategies were applied to water samples of different origin because of the simplicity of the matrix. More problems could be found for complex matrices, due to the components of the sample, incomplete separation of the elements form the matrix, loss of analytes if acid digestion or dry ashing is used, incomplete generation or collection of hydrides and difficulties for synthesis of NM phases such as Fe3O4 NPs in the presence of organic matter [26,67].

Main strategies using NPs for TXRF sample preparation focused on trapping and analyte preconcentration. In this section, main nanoparticle-assisted stabilization strategies found in the literature for this purpose were described. Table 2 summarizes selected applications.

Although Au NPs are potentially the particles with the most affinity to Hg, the problems previously mentioned related with the overlapping of both fluorescence [35,37,45] lines prevents the development of these applications for TXRF.

Other noble metal NPs like Pd and Ag NPs were adhered to the quartz reflector previously silanized and modified with thiol (-SH) groups for trapping volatile species by HS-TFME. Namely, Ag NPs were used for the preconcentration of Hg(0) and SeH2 [68] and Pd NPs for the preconcentration of Hg(0) [69], AsH3 [70], BiH3 and SbH3 [71].

In these works, most synthetic procedures involve Ag and Pd NPs due to the easiness and fastness of the synthesis. For the synthesis of Ag NPs, 1 mL of AgNO3 solution is added dropwise to 1 mL of NaBH4 (placed in an ice bath) in a ratio 1:6 of AgNO3:NaBH4. The reaction was completed within 6 min under agitation. For the synthesis of Pd NPs, a mixture H2O:ethanol (300 μL, 1:2 v/v) was added to Pd(NO3)2 (1.5 mg) and reaction was allowed to proceed for 10 min. Here, ethanol was used as reducing agent. Although this strategy provides good stability of the trapped volatile species and high sensitivity, the main drawback is the lengthy procedure for the treatment of the quartz reflectors to immobilize the noble metal NPs. Briefly, after careful washing (RBS 50® and 10% v/v HNO3) and hydrofobization [14], the quartz reflectors were functionalized with (3-mercaptopropyl)trimethoxysilane (MPTMS) (80 °C, 30 min) providing thiol surface groups susceptible of interacting with the NPs. For immobilization, an aliquot of freshly prepared Ag NPs or Pd NPs was added for covalent immobilization by reaction with the thiol groups. For Pd NPs, reflectors were left to air-dry, but for Ag NPs, drying in a hot plate at 60 °C was performed. Finally, the quartz reflectors were rinsed with water to remove the unbounded NPs. This strategy allows adhering the NPs to the quartz reflector so as to prevent leaching of the NPs.

After immobilization of Ag or Pd NPs onto quartz reflectors, the generation of volatile species is required in a closed vial or flow system to avoid losses. Aqueous samples solution can be chemically reduced using NaBH4 to generate SeH2 and Hg(0) [68], AsH3 [70], SbH3 and BiH3 [71] or can be submitted directly to ultraviolet irradiation to convert Hg(II) into Hg(0) [69].

The conditions applied for the generation of volatile species, e.g. Hg(0), SeH2, AsH3, SbH3, and BiH3 were very similar using NaBH4 as reducing agent under acidic conditions (15–20% v/v HCl) [68,70,71]. In addition, Hg(0) vapor can be also generated by photochemical reduction using diluted acetic acid as organic precursor. The photochemical vapor generation follows the principles of green chemistry in a better way, because a reducing agent like sodium borohydride is avoided.

The study of trapping mechanisms indicates that Hg(0) was retained by amalgamation, while SeH2, AsH3, SbH3 and BiH3 were retained by catalytic decomposition of the generated hydride onto the NPs [68–71].

The generation of volatile species can be performed using in-batch (discontinuous mode) or on-line system (continuous mode). However, the on-line system provided a sensitivity 10 times higher than the discontinuous system. The generated vapor is flushed to the thin film allowing its saturation. Böttger et al. [72] applied Ag NPs prepared in a similar way for trapping Hg in gas phase using different systems to provide a mercury atmosphere, such as a desiccator that provided a saturated atmosphere and a propylene box. The amalgamation time was very large, ca. 24 h. However, this application could be interesting to control Hg in the workplace air since the formed amalgam is very stable in time.

Main advantages of these strategies are the high enrichment factors achieved (175 534) and excellent LODs (sub-ppb to ppt level) especially for volatile elements like Hg. As mentioned above, the preparation time of quartz reflectors (cleaning + hydrofobization + addition of SH groups + NPs immobilization) could be lengthy, but 25 quartz reflectors can be prepared at a time. The time of extraction was in the range of 10–30 min.

An interesting strategy for Hg trapping and stabilization is the in situ preparation of Ag NPs on filter paper. A chemical reductant was only required for the synthesis of Ag NPs in the filter paper since Hg(II) was directly entrapped on modified filter paper without further reduction, hence simplifying the sample treatment. After trapping, the filter was fixed to the quartz reflector by adhesive tape and directly measured by TXRF. The strategy was tested by TXRF for screening of a wide number of analytes, but only works for Pb, Bi and especially for Hg. The latter element was also determined by a direct mercury analyzer following complete combustion of the filter paper [73].

Among the metallic NPs, magnetic NPs, such as magnetite (Fe3O4) provide the advantage of easy and fast separation of analyte-enriched NPs by the simple application of an external magnet for a few minutes. Filtration or centrifugation steps are unnecessary for the separation of the enriched magnetic NPs from the aqueous sample solution. Desorption of analytes from the magnetic NPs was not required either, since magnetic NPs can be directly analyzed by TXRF (10 μL) after dropping of the colloid onto the quartz reflector and evaporation of the aqueous residual phase. Washing of the magnetic NPs with ultrapure water was the only step required. Finally, Ga standard solution (10 μL of 1 mg L-1) is added as internal standard for calibration and quantification purposes.

Romero et al. [74] developed an integrated strategy named in situ ultrasound-assisted magnetite synthesis with simultaneous ion co-precipitation (in situ UAMS-SIC) that allows the simultaneous NPs synthesis and analyte trapping, when the procedure is developed directly on the sample solution. Eleven elements were preconcentrated in the magnetite NPs, including among them those with high volatility such as Hg, Ge, As and Se. Adsorption and occlusion were identified as the main co-precipitation mechanisms. Although satisfactory recoveries were achieved for Hg, Ge and As, the above strategy was ineffective for Se stabilization, which was lost by volatilization during the drying step yielding recoveries in the range of 65–76%.

It was observed that Hg was specially extracted when Au ions (at trace elements) were also present in the sample matrix [74]. Thus, the effect of the presence of certain noble metal ions such as Pd(II), Au(II), Ag(I) and Pt(IV) for in situ UAMS-SIC of Hg was assessed [67]. Hg could be determined at sub-ppt level using a direct mercury analyzer following the above strategy.

Since naked or unmodified magnetite NPs showed reduced extraction efficiency in comparison to other metal oxide NPs, improvements in the sorption ability of magnetite NPs was achieved following the preparation of core-shell NPs. MnO2 provides higher sorption efficiency in comparison with magnetite NPs, so the nanocomposite Fe3O4@MnO2 was synthesized [75]. The synthesis of the Fe3O4 NPs was the same as described above, and then, KMnO4 and polyethylene glycol were added to magnetite NPs dispersed in 30 mL of sample solution and sonicated with an ultrasonic probe for 30 s. This procedure allows the effective trapping of Se without losses by volatilization and an improved enrichment factors by 10 times as compared to naked Fe3O4 NPs. High enrichment factors were obtained for volatile elements such as Pb (540) and Se (402).

Main advantage of the in situ synthesis of magnetic NPs is the ability to integrate synthesis and extraction in one step along with easy dispersion of NPs and the avoidance of desorption, centrifugation and elution operations.

The appearance of a high intense signal of Fe (Kα: 6.4 keV and Kβ: 7.0 keV) and Fe sum peaks (12.8 and 14.0 keV) in the TXRF that may overlap other potential target analytes has been found as the main drawback of using direct analysis of enriched magnetic NPs on the quartz reflector. This fact can be observed in the spectra showed in Fig. 3A and B. Likewise, for the core-shell Fe3O4@MnO2, fluorescence signals of Mn (Kα: 5.9 keV and Kβ: 6.5 keV) and Fe appeared in the TXRF spectrum.

Multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) (6–9 nm diameter and 5 mm length) are commercially available, but oxidation with HNO3 is required before their use for extraction purposes [76,77]. Oxidation of CNTs is required so as to introduce carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surface, which are key for the sorption of these elements increasing their recoveries in comparison with raw CNTs. Following this strategy, Kocot et al. [65] developed a D-μ-SPE strategy using MWCNTs for extraction and preconcentration of volatile Pb and Cd as APDC chelates in water samples. The procedure mainly consists of the preparation of a suspension (20 mL) containing APDC (100 μL of 4 mg mL-1) and oxidized MWCNTs (100 μL of 2 mg mL-1) at pH 7. The mixture is shaken for 5 min and centrifuged (20 min, 4000 rpm). The aqueous phase is discarded and the sedimented phase is resuspended in a small volume (500 μL) containing standard solution of Y prior to TXRF analysis.

Although extraction of Cd and Pb occurred even without adding of chelating agent, extraction efficiency was improved in the presence of APDC. Different drying modes were tested, namely, a) laminar flow hood (at room temperature), b) IR-lamp and c) hot plate (at 80 °C). No losses by volatilization were observed showing the good stability of the APDC complexes.

Although centrifugation (or decantation) step is required after sorption of Cd and Pb in the MWCNTs to separate NPs from the aqueous solution. The metal enriched MWCNTs were resuspended and directly measured by TXRF. Thus, elution of target analytes, which is typically required for ICP-MS or ICP-OES techniques, was avoided when the analysis is performed by TXRF.

Graphene oxide (GO) does not provide selectivity towards trace metals, and hence, a competition is produced between target analytes and coexisting matrix components over active sites, especially in complex matrices [78]. Thus, there are two main alternatives to improve the selectivity of the GO, i.e. a) addition of a chelating agent and b) functionalization of adsorbent surface [78].

Sitko et al. [78] developed a novel adsorbent based on the grafting of MPTMS to modify the surface of GO. Thus, mercapto-modified graphene oxide (GO-SH) was used for adsorption of volatile species of As, Cd and Pb (among other elements) by D-μ-SPE. The main inconvenience is the fact that the preparation of GO-SH takes more than 17 h and requires reagents like graphite, H2SO4, NaNO3, KMnO4 and HCl.

GO was prepared by Hummers' method, which is usually tedious and time-consuming. For the modification of GO with thiol (-SH) groups, GO suspension in ethanol, prepared by sonication, was mixed with MPTMS and heated under reflux. This mixture was then centrifuged and the solid phase was redispersed by sonication, washed with ethanol (x8) and water (x8) and centrifuged. The solid GO-SH prepared in this way was dried and redispersed in water at a concentration of 5 mg mL-1 for further use.

For trapping the target analytes, a GO-SH suspension (200 μL of 5 mg mL-1) was added to the sample solution (pH = 5). After 10 min, the sample was filtered through a membrane filter being the GO-SH embedded in the filter. The trapped analytes must be eluted before analysis by sonication using diluted HNO3 (containing Y as an internal standard). Finally, a 10 μL aliquot of the eluate was analyzed by TXRF after drying onto the quartz reflector.

GO-SH reacts with As(III) in the presence of As(V), due to the presence of the mercapto group, thus showing its great selectivity. However, GO nanosheets can be lost during filtration or centrifugation due to their small size as well as softness and flexibility.

An alternative to avoid losses of CNTs or GO nanosheets during sample preparation, separation from the sample matrix or elution, can be the preparation of graphene membranes (Ø10 mm) as thin film that can be directly measured by TXRF. GO nanosheets, prepared by the Hummers' method, were used as membrane precursor. The preparation of graphene membranes takes more than 4 h and requires reagents like graphite, H2SO4, KNO3, KMnO4 and H2O2.

Graphene membranes were prepared by thermal reduction of GO. Briefly, an aliquot of GO aqueous dispersion was transferred by drop-casting onto glass supports (10 mm diameter) pre-heated at 90–100 °C. After drying, the deposited GO changed its color from dark yellow to black, thus confirming the thermal reduction to graphene. Finally, the glass support containing the membrane was placed on crystallizer beaker filled with ultrapure water. Highly hydrophobic graphene membranes were easily separated from glass supports [79].

Membranes having ca. 160 nm thickness are flexible and float on aqueous solution. In addition, they were stable for a long time (at least 30 days) being suitable for TFME. When the vial containing the sample solution and the membrane was submitted to magnetic stirring at high speed, a vortex was formed in the vial. The membrane adapts to the vortex and acquires a conical shape in the center of the vial.

Using graphene membranes, several elements prone to losses by volatilization, including Hg, As, Se and Pb were trapped as thermally stable APDC chelates following DI-TFME [80]. Formation of chelates with APDC was performed in aqueous solution (50 mL, pH = 3) and metal/metalloid chelates were extracted into the graphene membrane by hydrophobic interactions occurred between chelates and carbon-based ring structure of graphene [80]. The extraction is performed while the vial is stirring at 900 rpm for 10 min at room temperature. During stirring, the membrane was located in the center of the vortex formed in the aqueous solution. The stirring step favored the formation of APDC metal chelates and improved the mass transfer of the chelate from the aqueous solution to the graphene membrane. After the extraction, membranes can be easily collected employing a glass support and precision tweezers and deposited onto the quartz reflector and air-dried inside a laminar flow hood for measurement.

Another application for the graphene membranes was performed in headspace mode (i.e., HS-TFME) for trapping a volatile organic compound of Mn (methylcyclopentadienyl-Mn-tricarbonyl, MMT) that was used as an octane enhancer in fuels [81]. Although all the discussed works are related to volatile inorganic metal and metalloids, volatile organometallic compounds should also be considered since they are prone to volatilization during the drying step. In the case of MMT, the idea consisted of the generation of the vapor of the organomanganese compound by warming (55 °C) and stirring the sample (1600 rpm). The graphene membrane was placed in the headspace of the vial using the quartz reflector as the vial cap. The sample solution contained 20% m/v NaCl so as to enhance the transfer of MMT to the headspace. The upper part of the vial (where the membrane was placed) was cooled at 10 °C to improve the MMT sorption. A high enrichment factor was obtained (265) along with an excellent limit of detection (18 ng L-1).

The main advantage of carbon-based NMs is that they provide cleaner spectra as compared to metallic NPs with decreased background noise (Fig. 3A). This fact also may increase the LODs because the clean spectra allowed detecting metal or metalloid at lower concentration, which signal is not overlapped by fluorescence signals coming from NPs extraction phase. Among the different carbon-based sorbents, graphene membranes could be advantageous since in contrast to other sorbents, they do not require a separation step by centrifugation or filtration, which is troublesome with graphene nanosheets or carbon nanotubes.
Examples of obtained spectra using different NPs to show the provided background in TXRF are shown in Fig. 3A.

As can be seen (Fig. 3A), graphene membranes provided the lowest background signal, which facilitates the detection of elements at low concentration. For Pd and Ag, two peaks appeared at approximately 20 keV and 2 keV. Although fluorescence peaks for target analytes do not overlap with those of Ag or Pd, the increased background impairs detection limits. With Fe-containing NPs, e.g. naked Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4@MnO2 NPs, high peaks of Fe were observed at 6.4 keV for and sum peaks at 13 keV overlapping potential target elements.

Fig. 3B shows the energy of most common excitation sources (Mo, W and Mo/W alloys) and their useful energy window. Furthermore, fluorescence energy of target analytes and NPs used in the sample preparation procedure are presented. Thus, the k-radiation of a Mo X-ray tube used as excitation source (17.5 keV) can be observed, indicating that useful window for different elements from 5 to 15 keV, i.e. Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Au, Hg, Tl, Pb, Bi, Ga, Ge, As, Se. For the X-ray tube of W, the element window is wider (non-useful from 30 to 40 keV), because the white radiation of W is 59.3 keV allowing the determination of elements like Sb and Cd, which could not be detected with the Mo X-ray source.

Additionally, elements that cannot be determined using metal NPs due to potential overlapping of fluorescence signal depending on the NPs used and the X-ray tube forming part the TXRF instrument are summarized in Table 3 . For Pd and Ag, the two peaks appearing at approximately 20 keV and 2 keV did not produce disturbance for Mo X-ray tube, but, they can hamper the determination of Sn, Sb and Cd with the W X-ray tube. With Fe-containing NPs, elements like Mn, Co and Ni cannot be determined and with Fe and Mn NPs, Cr cannot be determined for Mo X-ray tube either.

A large variety of strategies were developed in the literature to prevent analyte losses by volatility during sample preparation before TXRF analysis or even during storage of the analyte trapped onto the quartz reflector.

Hg amalgamation with Au and Ag films and Hg complexation were the first strategies to reduce volatilization losses. As discussed, other effective possibilities were adsorption onto polymer membranes made of Nafion®, immobilization of ligands onto quartz reflectors for analyte trapping and stabilization. Together with Hg, these strategies have been also successfully applied for other volatile species, such as Se, As, Pb and Cd. Furthermore, complexation procedures can be combined with microextraction techniques, e.g. DLLME, to enhance further the sensitivity of the technique. Besides, the classical slurry sampling approach proved to be suitable to avoid also losses of volatile species, such as Se, in comparison with extraction procedures using concentrated strong acids.

During the last years, there has been a remarkable increase in the use of NMs as an alternative to conventional procedures for preconcentration and thermal stabilization of different volatile elements. Carbon-based NMs may be more ecological than metallic NPs, but metal catalysts are usually required for synthesis for example for CNTs.

A centrifugation step was required for phase separation (nanomaterial from the aqueous phase) of CNTs and filtration was needed for modified GO-SH nanosheets followed by analyte elution. On the contrary, graphene membranes can be directly measured after analyte trapping without any further operation. Metallic NPs, especially magnetite NPs, gave rise to high intensity fluorescence signals of Fe that may overlap fluorescence signals of target analytes with similar fluorescence energy. Apart from efficient trapping of volatile species with Ag NPs and Pd NPs, which avoids their loss during sample drying, improved LODs and enrichment factors are achieved. Application of nanomaterials yielding low background along with avoidance of additional operations such as filtration, centrifugation, decantation or elution should provide simple and efficient methods for trace element analysis by TXRF.

Undoubtedly, novel TXRF equipment having to possibility of changing the X-ray tube in the same instrument, may improve or increase the application range of TXRF. Thus, depending on the NPs used, one X-ray tube may be more adequate than the other. Over the next years, significant advances are expected in the development of benchtop TXRF instrumentation, which combined to novel sample treatment approaches could be competitive with hyphenated techniques for trace and ultratrace analysis, such as ICP-based techniques.
